Please describe your research career to date
Career to date
My career experiences have reinforced my desire to pursue a career as a leading clinical academic in
Rheumatology. I completed my undergraduate medical studies and an intercalated degree at the University of
Cambridge, earning yearly academic prizes. My Foundation Training took place at the Countess of Chester
Hospital, followed by an MSc in Immunology at the University of Oxford. Subsequently, I secured an NIHR
Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) with run-through specialty training in Rheumatology. Currently, I am an ST5-
level trainee in the South London programme.
Research Experience
I developed a passion for research during my intercalated degree with a project investigating the effect of
propranolol on the reconsolidation of conditioned fear memories. This involved animal model work, laboratory work
and a literature review, resulting in a second-author publication in Neuroscience.
I was first drawn to a career in rheumatology during my Fo

rences.
Throughout training, I have developed my expertise in medical education; authoring rheumatology-themed
questions for nationwide educational platforms and delivering a pan-London session on “Rheumatology Spot
Diagnoses” that was attended by 315 doctors. I continue to teach regularly.
Impact of Fellowship
Sarcoidosis is an under-researched condition associated with significant mortality and morbidity. If awarded an
NIHR doctoral research fellowship, I aim to develop methods to identify patients with sarcoidosis at high risk of
severe disease. My research will provide objective clinical and radiological measurements of disease activity,
guiding clinical trial design on a global scale. Improved identification of patients with severe disease will guide
referral to specialist centres and prescription of second-line immunosuppressive therapies, improving patient
quality of life and reducing healthcare burdens.
This fellowship would enable me to pursue my career goal of becoming a research leader in the field of
sarcoidosis. The skills that I gain during this research training programme will put me in a strong position to submit
a successful application for an NIHR Clinical Lecture

Proposed training and development programme
1. Academic training and courses
To advance my knowledge and skills in epidemiological analysis, I will complete the ‘Advanced Course in
Epidemiological Analysis’ hosted by the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This two-week course
covers a range of epidemiological methodologies in Stata, which will be extremely useful for Phase 1 of my project
and my future research career.
As a KCL student, I will take advantage of the abundance of learning opportunities. To develop skills in
programming, using Python, I will complete the KCL module “Introduction to Programming”. To further my
understanding of deep learning, I will complete the “KCL Masters modules on machine learning and advanced
machine learning”, focusing on the modules in medical imaging.
I will also attend the international “Summer School on Deep Learning for Medical Imaging”. This one-week course
covers the basic and advanced aspects of deep learning, which will prove extremely useful for Phase 2 of my
study.
I will attend the British Medical Association (BMA) Foundation and Advanced Level Leadership Programmes.
These are free to BMA members and cover topics includin

network of epidemiologists and statisticians to support me during my Fellowship.

I will attend conferences in each year of my Fellowship, allowing me to present my research findings and network
with rheumatology and relevant medical specialists. These will include British Society for Rheumatology Annual

specialists in rheumatology, cardiology and respiratory medicine. Through this placement, I will learn from world-
leading academic clinicians and build strong collaborative links that will be extremely useful for my ongoing career.

undation Training. During this time, I audited the local
management of inflammatory arthritis and led a pilot trial on the efficacy of dietary intervention for gastroparesis in
patients with systemic sclerosis. I presented these projects at the British Society for Rheumatology (BSR) annual
conference. I also furthered my skills in critical appraisal by co-authoring a review article on post-transplantation
diabetes mellitus.
My MSc in Immunology enhanced my understanding of basic science and critical appraisal. During this time, I
undertook a research project investigating the effect of talarozole on inflammatory signalling in chondrocytes. This
involved laboratory work (chondrocyte isolation, culture and stimulation and Western blotting) and data analysis.
My primary NIHR ACF research project was a feasibility trial investigating the effect of different biological
medications on pain outcomes in participants with rheumatoid arthritis. I consented participants for recruitment,
conducted study visits and provided supervision to the research nurses and research fellows; enhancing my
leadership and management skills. I also performed data analysis, using SPSS and GraphPad. Although
par

ticipant recruitment was lower than expected due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the work was published and I
was proud to be a co-author.
The COVID-19 pandemic redirected my ACF research to studying the clinical outcomes of patients with
autoimmune conditions who developed COVID-19. During this work, I submitted applications for ethical approval,
conducted trial visits, performed laboratory work (including PBMC isolation, ELISAs and ELISpots) and analysed
trial data. I presented my findings at national and international conferences.
During my ACF, I authored three literature reviews, two case reports and a book chapter, improving my skills in
scientific writing. I was the first author of a book introducing clinical research concepts to medical professionals. I
wrote chapters on statistics, clinical study design and ethical and governance considerations in research. I also
delivered an oral presentation at the BSR Case-Based Conference and completed the PGCert in Healthcare
Research Skills and Methods at St George’s University with modules in statistics, research methods and critical
appraisal.
I am a current member of the BSR working group for the ANCA-associated vasculitis guidelines.

evidence.text:
Background
Sarcoidosis is a disease that causes lumps called “granulomas” to form in the body. These granulomas cause
inflammation and organ damage. Sarcoidosis is rare, but it is important to understand because it can cause death.
The problem
Sarcoidosis affects patients in very different ways. In some patients, sarcoidosis is very mild and gets better on its
own. In other patients, sarcoidosis can be deadly. It is very difficult to predict which patients will develop severe
disease. It’s also hard to predict which patients will die from their disease. Therefore, doctors struggle to know
which patients need treatment. There haven’t been many clinical trials in sarcoidosis and we don’t know which
treatment is best.
Sarcoidosis can affect any part of the body. PET-CT is a special type of medical scan which can find affected
organs and guide treatment. Doctors at different hospitals use PET-CT scans in different ways. If we knew how
best to use PET-CT scans, they could tell us which patients are at the greatest risk of severe disease.
Aim of my research
The aim of my research is to identify which patients are at risk of severe sarcoidosis. We want to know how factors
such as age, sex

evidence.text:
, and ethnicity affect the risk of severe disease. We also want to know how the pattern of disease
on PET-CT scans affects the patients’ risk.
My research will help:
 Find patients who need early treatment. This will reduce the risk of organ damage and death.
Identify patients who qualify for clinical trials of new treatments. This will drive further research into

sarcoidosis, with the hope of finding new effective medications.
 Target resources to groups of people who need them the most. This will help NHS managers to allocate
healthcare services effectively.
Design and methods
I will divide my research into three parts.
Phase 1: We will collect evidence from medical records in GP practices. We will do this using a secure
system called OpenSAFELY. We will look at patients with sarcoidosis and compare those with mild and severe
disease. We will look at their age, sex, ethnicity. We will also consider their other health conditions and pattern of
sarcoidosis. We will determine whether there are any factors that increase the risk of death or organ damage.
Phase 2: We will train an artificial intelligence (AI) model. It will measure disease in the heart and lungs on PET-
CT scans.
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
We will test the model to make sure that it is accurate.
Phase 3: We will look at patients with sarcoidosis over a one-year follow-up period. All patients will have a PET-
CT at baseline. They will do this as part of their routine clinical care. We will use the AI model to find PET-CT scan
features that predict severe disease one year later.
Patient and public involvement
I have worked with the charity Sarcoidosis UK to speak to different patients. I conducted interviews and a focus
group to understand the impact of sarcoidosis. Six patient representatives will continue to guide the project. They
will help to design the study and analyse the results. Patient input will ensure that research focuses on benefiting
patients.
Dissemination
My patient panel will help ensure that my results reach as many people as possible.
Many approaches will be used:
 Plain English summaries of all publications.
 Newsletters of outputs, which can be disseminated through charities including Sarcoidosis UK.
 Social media outputs, including Facebook posts.
 Public engagement events, organised in conjunction with Sarcoidosis UK.

Identification of demographic predictors of severe sarcoidosis should allow appropriate targeting of healthcare

with an aggressive disease phenotype, I aim to reduce the burden of emergency department admissions and

Review of the existing evidence - how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Sarcoidosis is associated with high mortality rates. About 5% of patients with sarcoidosis die from their
disease, and observational studies indicate that mortality rates are doubled compared to matched controls9, 10,11.
Currently, we lack methods to predict which patients will die from sarcoidosis.
Sarcoidosis is an under-researched condition and disproportionately affects groups that are under-
represented in research

studies, including people of Black ethnicity and those with socioeconomic
deprivation12,13,14,15. The lifetime risk for sarcoidosis is three times higher in African-American than in Caucasian
Americans (0.85% versus 2.4%)13. Furthermore, Afro-Caribbean ethnicity has been associated with organ-
prognosis16,17. research12.

The prevalence, incidence and mortality rates from sarcoidosis are increasing over time10,11. Our
preliminary data, using Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD), demonstrates a four-fold increase in the
prevalence of sarcoidosis over the last 20 years (Figure 1). There were an estimated 3,450 new diagnoses in
England in 2022 (Figure 2).
Newer imaging modalities are increasing our understanding of sarcoidosis and organ-threatening disease.
18F-FDG PET-CT is a functional imaging modality that is used in the diagnosis and monitoring of patients with
sarcoidosis (Figure 3). Despite its commonplace use, we do not have validated methods to quantify disease
severity on PET-CT18.
The optimal treatment of sarcoidosis is uncertain. Trials in sarcoidosis a

re limited by disease rarity,
heterogeneity and a lack of standardised trial outcome measures19. Current guidelines are based on expert
opinion and small uncontrolled trials9,20,21. Corticosteroids are the first-line treatment option but are associated with
significant toxicity; observational studies link an increased dose of corticosteroids with an increased number of
non-sarcoidosis-related emergency department visits, recurrent flares and higher mortality rates22,23,24. Steroid-
sparing agents are effective treatments but are underutilised, especially outside of tertiary units6.
There is an unmet need for better treatments in the management of sarcoidosis. Our systematic review
highlighted the lack of high-quality RCTs investigating the efficacy of second-line immunosuppressive agents in
sarcoidosis. As newer treatment options are emerging, there is a need to identify patients with severe disease who
are most likely to benefit from them.
My research aims to characterise risk factors for severe sarcoidosis. This work will identify patients who would
benefit most from second-line immunosuppressive therapies.

Phase 1: Clinical and demographic predictors for poor outcomes (0-12 months)
Overview
Phase 1 is a nationwide cohort study comparing the rate of severe disease outcomes between patients with and
without predictors of interest.
Background
In American and Scandinavian populations, risk factors for poor prognosis in sarcoidosis include socio-economic

target healthcare resources and minimise inequalities in healthcare. This phase will investigate, characterise and

Methods
The following definitions will be used throughout this phase of the project:
Index diagnosis of sarcoidosis – A first-ever diagnostic code of sarcoidosis between 2004 and 2024, in people
aged 18 and above, permanently registered with a GP in England that uses TPP and EMIS electronic health
record software.
Severe disease - Disease resulting in hospitalisation, end-organ damage or mortality. Patients will also be
considered to have severe disease if they require treatment escalation or a cumulative lifetime prednisolone dose
greater than 500mg.
Predictors of severe disease - Demographic and clinical factors that may predict a severe disease outcome.
Outcomes of severe disease:
Outcomes are defined as events that occur at or after the time of diagnosis of sarcoidosis. They will include;
hospitalisation and end-organ damage, treatment escalation, corticosteroid burd

be expressed as age-adjusted standardised Mortality Ratios, using indirect standardisation,
according to England mortality data using UK-based life tables as the reference data. Mortality rates will be
compared between groups with different predictors of interest.
Statistical analysis plan
Analyses will be performed to identify factors/predictors associated with the above outcomes including
age, gender, ethnicity, geographical region, socio-economic deprivation, organ involvement and comorbidities.
Justifications for their inclusion are outlined in Table 2.
A competing risk survival model will be performed to estimate the cumulative incidence of each outcome at 1, 2
and 5 years. The start date will be the date of the first recorded diagnosis of sarcoidosis. The end date will be the
time of the outcome of interest. Multiple models will be performed, using the following outcomes of interest:
 Time to death
 Time to end-organ damage, including sub-analyses on organ systems
 Time to treatment with second or third-line immunosuppressive medications
 Time to treatment with a cumulative corticosteroid dose of more than 500mg.
Sample size calculation
I have a fixed population for my st

s, with estimates ranging between 89% to 100%
across observational studies18.
PET-CT carries significant advantages over non-functional imaging modalities. It can differentiate active
inflammation from “burnt-out” disease. PET-CT can also quantify disease activity by providing objective measures
of 18F-FDG uptake using the Standardised Uptake Values (SUV). Maximum SUV (SUVmax) correlates with
disease activity, including patient-reported symptoms27, FVC18 and cardiac events28,29.
Benefits of AI
AI is being used increasingly for automating time-consuming medical imaging tasks and reducing inter-observer
variability. Convolutional neural network (CNN)-based approaches are a branch of deep learning that are finding
application in the analysis of medical images. CNN approaches are highly effective in imaging tasks such as
recognition, object detection and segmentation and studies suggest that they can reach near-human performance
in well-defined tasks30.
CNNs have proven highly effective in classifying uptake on PET-CT in cancer settings30. Studies have used CNNs
to localise, classify and quantify foci of malignant disease31. In studies in lymphoma patients, CNNs have been
trained to ca

s. For each focus
of increased 18F-FDG uptake, the classification and anatomical location (i.e. organ) will be recorded. The expert
annotations will allow training of the CNN and testing of sensitivity and specificity for correct classification as
consistent or inconsistent with active sarcoidosis.
CNN training and validation
PET-CTs will be randomly split into three independent subsets; validation (20%), training (60%) and testing (20%).
A validation set will be used to select model hyperparameters and determine the best-trained CNN. The testing
subset will be used to assess the final performance of the model33.
Sample Size Calculation
At KCH, 10-20 PET-CTs are performed in patients with sarcoidosis every month. Using a conservative estimate of
10 PET-CTs per month over the last five years, 600 PET-CT scans will be available to test, train and validate the
algorithm.
Sample size calculations are not routinely performed during the development of deep-learning algorithms. The
inclusion of a higher number of scans is usually associated with improved CNN performance. Sample size will be
limited by the feasibility and time required for manual delineation of FDG activity. We aim to incl

UVmax has been shown to correlate with sarcoidosis disease activity, this is poorly characterised and
prospective studies are lacking32. SUVmax is a detail-lacking measurement that does not describe the total
quantity or pattern of uptake. The correlation between objective measurements of PET-CT activity and clinical
outcomes could standardise PET-CT interpretation. This has the potential to standardise clinical trial design and
clinical treatment decisions.
Methods
I will perform an observational prospective study of participants with biopsy-confirmed sarcoidosis with PET-CT
performed at baseline. Clinical outcome markers will be measured at 0- and 12 months post-recruitment.
Participant recruitment
Inclusion criteria
 Biopsy confirmed sarcoidosis.
 Plan for PET-CT under routine NHS care, ensuring no additional radiation exposure on recruitment into
this trial.
Exclusion criteria and justification
 Patients with localised neurosarcoidosis will be excluded from the study as the results will not be
generalisable, due to the limited access to brain-PET-CT imaging.
 Pregnancy or post-partum, due to the harmful risks of radiation during this period.

evidence.text:
What is the problem being addressed?
Sarcoidosis is an inflammatory condition characterised by granuloma formation in organs, most commonly the
lungs and lymphatic system. Its clinical presentation can vary markedly, from mild and self-limiting, to severe
organ damage and death1. Sarcoidosis is associated with an increased risk of mortality, largely due to respiratory
or cardiac involvement2,3. It is a rare disease and therefore research into sarcoidosis is often neglected.
Throughout this application, I have worked closely with patients and gained an appreciation of the enormous
impact of a diagnosis of sarcoidosis on the individual. Not only is the condition associated with burdensome
symptoms, treatment options are limited and often lead to significant side effects. Our preliminary data shows that
about 22,000 people are living with sarcoidosis in England. These patients represent an underserved population in
urgent need of evidence-based improvements in their care.
Research into sarcoidosis is often underfunded due to a lack of interest from pharmaceutical companies due to
disease rarity. Corticosteroids remain the first-line therapeutic option but are associated with significa

rcoidosis is managed by many medical specialities, including rheumatology, respiratory, cardiology, neurology,
ophthalmology and dermatology teams. Interviews with patient representatives highlighted the disjointed care that
patients with sarcoidosis often receive and the lack of interdisciplinary work in some cases.
My research will provide information on how different patterns of organ involvement predict outcomes and this will
guide us on how care should be joined up. My research will be relevant to guideline developers on the standards
of care for multi-system sarcoidosis. I will work with relevant societies, e.g. the British Thoracic Society, the British
Society of Rheumatology and the European Society of Cardiology, to disseminate my work and ensure that it
translates to improved patient outcomes.
Impacts on wider healthcare provision
By clarifying which patients will benefit from second-line immunosuppression, my research has the potential to
reduce steroid-related side effects, improve resource allocation, improve patient outcomes, reduce disability and
reduce costs for commissioners. As an example, our data estimates that 1.5% of patients with sarcoidosis go on to
require

home oxygen therapy. This is an expensive therapy and the total annual cost for home oxygen in the UK is
approximately £120 million8. Even small reductions in the number who require this treatment would significantly
benefit healthcare resource utilisation.
Identification of demographic predictors of severe sarcoidosis should allow appropriate targeting of healthcare
resources to those who are most in need. In the longer term, through the identification and treatment of patients
with an aggressive disease phenotype, I aim to reduce the burden of emergency department admissions and
hospitalisation of these patients. This will ease pressures on the already-stretched NHS services.
Review of the existing evidence - how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Sarcoidosis is associated with high mortality rates. About 5% of patients with sarcoidosis die from their
disease, and observational studies indicate that mortality rates are doubled compared to matched controls9, 10,11.
Currently, we lack methods to predict which patients will die from sarcoidosis.
Sarcoidosis is an under-researched condition and disproportionately affects groups that are under-
represented in research

societal outputs:
My research will inform the design of clinical trials of immunosuppressive medications for sarcoidosis on a global
scale. My research will allow non-expert clinicians to identify patients at risk of life-threatening sarcoidosis, who
should be considered for referral to specialist centres and treatment with second-line immunosuppressive
medications. This will improve the care of patients with sarcoidosis, reducing hospitalisations and death.
Impact statement:
My goal is to produce robust and objective methods that are capable of accurately predicting sarcoidosis patients
at risk of severe disease. By identifying high-risk patients, I hope to identify:
 Patients who should be referred to specialist tertiary centres for treatment.
 Patients who will benefit from second-line immunosuppressive therapies, reducing corticosteroid burden
and the risk of flare when steroids are tapered.
 Patients who should be recruited into clinical trials of novel therapies.

management of inflammatory arthritis and led a pilot trial on the efficacy of dietary intervention for gastroparesis in
patients with systemic sclerosis. I presented these projects at the British Society for Rheumatology (BSR) annual
conference. I also furthered my skills in critical appraisal by co-authoring a review article on post-transplantation
diabetes mellitus.
My MSc in Immunology enhanced my understanding of basic science and critical appraisal. During this time, I
undertook a research project investigating the effect of talarozole on inflammatory signalling in chondrocytes. This
involved laboratory work (chondrocyte isolation, culture and stimulation and Western blotting) and data analysis.
My primary NIHR ACF research project was a feasibility trial investigating the effect of different biological
medications on pain outcomes in participants with rheumatoid arthritis. I consented participants for recruitment,
conducted study visits and provided supervision to the research nurses and research fellows; enhancing my
leadership and management skills. I also performed data analysis, using SPSS and GraphPad. Although
par

ticipant recruitment was lower than expected due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the work was published and I
was proud to be a co-author.
The COVID-19 pandemic redirected my ACF research to studying the clinical outcomes of patients with
autoimmune conditions who developed COVID-19. During this work, I submitted applications for ethical approval,
conducted trial visits, performed laboratory work (including PBMC isolation, ELISAs and ELISpots) and analysed
trial data. I presented my findings at national and international conferences.
During my ACF, I authored three literature reviews, two case reports and a book chapter, improving my skills in
scientific writing. I was the first author of a book introducing clinical research concepts to medical professionals. I
wrote chapters on statistics, clinical study design and ethical and governance considerations in research. I also
delivered an oral presentation at the BSR Case-Based Conference and completed the PGCert in Healthcare
Research Skills and Methods at St George’s University with modules in statistics, research methods and critical
appraisal.
I am a current member of the BSR working group for the ANCA-associated vasculitis guidelines.

I have attended
working group meetings, completed a systematic literature review, written consensus statements and drafted the
final document. Throughout this process, I have worked closely with academic experts at leading UK centres,
highlighting the importance of networking and collaboration in research.
Research Experience in Sarcoidosis
My rheumatology training sparked an interest in multi-system conditions, particularly sarcoidosis. Whilst caring for
patients with sarcoidosis, I became aware of the paucity of research into this condition, when compared to other
inflammatory diseases. Despite being associated with significant morbidity and mortality, I noticed a significant
lack of epidemiological and trial data in this area.
Working with Dr Bechman and Professor Galloway, I performed a systematic review of biological medications in
sarcoidosis. Only nine RCTs were identified, highlighting an unmet research need. This work was presented at the
European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR) Congress and I have written a manuscript that is
currently under submission. More recently, we have used population-wide primary care data to describe the
epidemiology of sarcoido

The prevalence, incidence and mortality rates from sarcoidosis are increasing over time10,11. Our
preliminary data, using Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD), demonstrates a four-fold increase in the
prevalence of sarcoidosis over the last 20 years (Figure 1). There were an estimated 3,450 new diagnoses in
England in 2022 (Figure 2).
Newer imaging modalities are increasing our understanding of sarcoidosis and organ-threatening disease.
18F-FDG PET-CT is a functional imaging modality that is used in the diagnosis and monitoring of patients with
sarcoidosis (Figure 3). Despite its commonplace use, we do not have validated methods to quantify disease
severity on PET-CT18.

Participants will be recruited from KCH. Participants will be identified from sarcoidosis MDTs and be recruited
during routine clinic appointments. Recruitment will adhere to Good Clinical Practice guidelines.
Type and content of participant information materials
Before recruitment, all participants will receive Patient Information Leaflets (PILs). PILs will be co-written with
patient representatives. They will outline what the study involves, including possible risks and what happens after
the study finishes. PILs will explain that recruitment is optional and that consent can be withdrawn at any point.
Equality, Diversity and Inclusion for Study Participants
Participants will be recruited from KCH which serves an ethnically diverse population. Demographic data will be
collected for all patients, including data on protected characteristics. I have created an Equality Impact
Assessment to identify and address factors that may limit the recruitment of underserved populations into the
study. To ensure adequate recruitment of participants from Black Asian and Minority Ethnic (BAME) backgrounds,
I follow the NIHR Toolkit for increasing the participation of BAME groups in healthcare and

.
I will work with a patient representative of BAME background, to identify factors that may limit recruitment of ethnic
minorities into the observational trial. This patient will be offered an honorary research contract and has agreed to
support my research proposal throughout.
During Phase 1, participants will be invited to discuss predictors and outcomes of interest and will aid design of the
study protocol. In Phase 2, I will invite patient representatives to join meetings with Physicists and Radiologists to
design the deep learning model to measure disease activity on PET-CT.
The patient advisory group will join the stakeholder meeting to develop the observational trial including measures
of disease severity, including the inclusion of different symptom questionnaires (Phase 3). We will work together to
ensure that the study is acceptable to patients. The patient advisory group will support the writing of patient
information leaflets and lay summaries.

To advance my knowledge and skills in epidemiological analysis, I will complete the ‘Advanced Course in

study.

questions for nationwide educational platforms and delivering a pan-London session on “Rheumatology Spot
Diagnoses” that was attended by 315 doctors. I continue to teach regularly.
Impact of Fellowship
Sarcoidosis is an under-researched condition associated with significant mortality and morbidity. If awarded an
NIHR doctoral research fellowship, I aim to develop methods to identify patients with sarcoidosis at high risk of
severe disease. My research will provide objective clinical and radiological measurements of disease activity,
guiding clinical trial design on a global scale. Improved identification of patients with severe disease will guide
referral to specialist centres and prescription of second-line immunosuppressive therapies, improving patient
quality of life and reducing healthcare burdens.
This fellowship would enable me to pursue my career goal of becoming a research leader in the field of
sarcoidosis. The skills that I gain during this research training programme will put me in a strong position to submit
a successful application for an NIHR Clinical Lecture

disease activity in sarcoidosis (Phase 2); the third, describing the relationship between sarcoidosis activity on PET-
CT and contemporary disease activity and patient outcome (Phase 3). I will target journals with wide audiences,
ensuring health professionals from multiple clinical specialties can access my research outcomes.
I will present my findings at rheumatology and thoracic conferences. I will use the links that I have built with
cardiology and respiratory colleagues to disseminate findings to a broad range of medical audiences.
Patient outputs:
I have engaged the support of the Sarcoidosis UK Charity as a collaborator to support dissemination strategies to
patients and the wider population. My PPI panel will lead on dissemination strategies that will include:
1. Plain English Summaries to accompany all publications
2. Social media summaries of outputs (including the Facebook Sarcoidosis UK page)
3. Public engagement events (via BSR, EULAR, RCP and Sarcoidosis UK patient council)
4. Newletters via the Sarcoidosis UK charity
Healthcare and

societal outputs:
My research will inform the design of clinical trials of immunosuppressive medications for sarcoidosis on a global
scale. My research will allow non-expert clinicians to identify patients at risk of life-threatening sarcoidosis, who
should be considered for referral to specialist centres and treatment with second-line immunosuppressive
medications. This will improve the care of patients with sarcoidosis, reducing hospitalisations and death.
Impact statement:
My goal is to produce robust and objective methods that are capable of accurately predicting sarcoidosis patients
at risk of severe disease. By identifying high-risk patients, I hope to identify:
 Patients who should be referred to specialist tertiary centres for treatment.
 Patients who will benefit from second-line immunosuppressive therapies, reducing corticosteroid burden
and the risk of flare when steroids are tapered.
 Patients who should be recruited into clinical trials of novel therapies.
By achieving the above, my work will reduce symptom burden and the risk of end-organ damage and mortality. My
work will also drive objective methods of RCT design and inclusion criteria, improving the efficiency o

The prevalence, incidence and mortality rates from sarcoidosis are increasing over time10,11. Our
preliminary data, using Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD), demonstrates a four-fold increase in the
prevalence of sarcoidosis over the last 20 years (Figure 1). There were an estimated 3,450 new diagnoses in
England in 2022 (Figure 2).
Newer imaging modalities are increasing our understanding of sarcoidosis and organ-threatening disease.
18F-FDG PET-CT is a functional imaging modality that is used in the diagnosis and monitoring of patients with
sarcoidosis (Figure 3). Despite its commonplace use, we do not have validated methods to quantify disease
severity on PET-CT18.

Participants will be recruited from KCH. Participants will be identified from sarcoidosis MDTs and be recruited
during routine clinic appointments. Recruitment will adhere to Good Clinical Practice guidelines.
Type and content of participant information materials
Before recruitment, all participants will receive Patient Information Leaflets (PILs). PILs will be co-written with
patient representatives. They will outline what the study involves, including possible risks and what happens after
the study finishes. PILs will explain that recruitment is optional and that consent can be withdrawn at any point.
Equality, Diversity and Inclusion for Study Participants
Participants will be recruited from KCH which serves an ethnically diverse population. Demographic data will be
collected for all patients, including data on protected characteristics. I have created an Equality Impact
Assessment to identify and address factors that may limit the recruitment of underserved populations into the
study. To ensure adequate recruitment of participants from Black Asian and Minority Ethnic (BAME) backgrounds,
I follow the NIHR Toolkit for increasing the participation of BAME groups in healthcare and

.
I will work with a patient representative of BAME background, to identify factors that may limit recruitment of ethnic
minorities into the observational trial. This patient will be offered an honorary research contract and has agreed to
support my research proposal throughout.
During Phase 1, participants will be invited to discuss predictors and outcomes of interest and will aid design of the
study protocol. In Phase 2, I will invite patient representatives to join meetings with Physicists and Radiologists to
design the deep learning model to measure disease activity on PET-CT.
The patient advisory group will join the stakeholder meeting to develop the observational trial including measures
of disease severity, including the inclusion of different symptom questionnaires (Phase 3). We will work together to
ensure that the study is acceptable to patients. The patient advisory group will support the writing of patient
information leaflets and lay summaries.

Aims and objectives
My research plan has 3 phases:
1. To identify demographic and clinical risk factors for adverse outcomes in patients with sarcoidosis.
2. To develop a deep-learning model capable of quantifying sarcoidosis activity in the heart and lungs on
PET-CT.
3. To identify PET-CT correlates of disease activity and future disease outcomes.
Methods (timelines of delivery)
Phase 1 (0-12 months): Using the nationwide OpenSAFELY platform, I will analyse clinical and demographic risk
factors for adverse outcomes, including end-organ damage, hospitalisation and death. Regression models will be
used to correlate predictors of interest with adverse outcomes.
Phase 2 (6-18 months): Using PET-CT performed in patients with confirmed sarcoidosis, I will develop a novel
deep-learning algorithm to quantify and characterise disease burden and pattern on PET-CT, focusing on uptake
in the heart and lungs.

PET-CT performed at baseline. Using the deep-learning algorithm developed in Phase 2, I will use regression

Key aims and objectives
My key study phases, aims and objectives are:
Phase 1: Characterise the clinical and demographic predictors of adverse outcomes in patients with sarcoidosis
(0-12 months).
Phase 2: Develop a deep-learning model capable of localising and quantifying disease activity on 18F-FDG PET-
CT (6-18 months).
Phase 3: Correlate activity on PET-CT with contemporary disease activity and disease outcome (12-36 months)
Project Plan
Phase 1: Clinical and demographic predictors for poor outcomes (0-12 months)
Overview
Phase 1 is a nationwide cohort study comparing the rate of severe disease outcomes between patients with and
without predictors of interest.
